body_txt: Ferrets in fiction are generally energetic characters. Being really hyperactive, they're depicted as Genki Girls or Keets with boundless amounts of energy. This often also means they have short attention spans , or in the case of Talking Animals , they might be a Motor Mouth . This is Truth in Television . Ferrets are very lively and curious pets, even more so than cats. They're prone to doing a "weasel war dance" when particularly excited. Some ferret owners will even struggle to keep up with their ferrets because they're too energetic for them. Of course, this hyperactivity decreases as they get older, and they might not be as playful at old age (usually around 5–6) as they were when they were kits. Ferret-legging , a sport in the north of England, exploits this trope, as it originated from coal-miners putting lively ferrets down their trouser legs and having a race to see whose Ferret would exit first. It's a weird sport, for sure. Sub-Trope of Animal Stereotype and Weasel Mascot . Compare Playful Otter for the aquatic equivalent and Cheetah Speedsta for another example of an energetic animal. Contrast Wicked Weasel , Bad Ol' Badger , Rascally Raccoon , and Smelly Skunk for negative depictions of other musteloids.
